Name: gpu_inference_optimization
Title: Kernel-Level Throughput Engineering for Efficient Machine Learning Inference on Commodity GPUs
Short Hypothesis: By optimizing kernel-level throughput and memory-bandwidth utilization, we can achieve significant speedups in machine learning inference on commodity GPUs without sacrificing accuracy.
Related Work: Previous work has focused on optimizing machine learning models for inference, but few have explored the potential of kernel-level throughput engineering and hardware-faithful evaluation. Our proposal builds on the idea of optimizing machine learning inference for specific hardware platforms, but with a focus on commodity GPUs and a rigorous, measurement-based approach.
Abstract: This proposal presents a novel approach to optimizing machine learning inference on commodity GPUs. By applying kernel-level throughput engineering and hardware-faithful evaluation, we aim to achieve significant speedups in inference times without sacrificing accuracy. Our approach involves optimizing fused CUDA/Triton kernels, memory-bandwidth utilization, KV-cache layout, and tensor-core occupancy, and evaluating the performance using detailed metrics such as occupancy, achieved bandwidth, warp stall reasons, and arithmetic intensity. We will conduct experiments using real-world models and datasets, and report results in terms of wall-clock latency, tokens-per-second, and energy per token.
Experiments: Optimize kernel-level throughput for a range of machine learning models, including convolutional neural networks and recurrent neural networks
Evaluate the performance of the optimized kernels using detailed metrics such as occupancy, achieved bandwidth, warp stall reasons, and arithmetic intensity
Conduct A/B latency races under fixed accuracy budgets, reporting p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token
Investigate the impact of quantization, sparsity, and layer-skipping on the latency-accuracy Pareto front on commodity GPUs
Risk Factors and Limitations: The optimization process may be time-consuming and require significant expertise in CUDA and GPU architecture
The approach may not generalize to all types of machine learning models or hardware platforms
The evaluation metrics may not capture all aspects of performance, and additional metrics may be needed to fully characterize the benefits and limitations of the approach
